questions.question: My sister ___ tennis every Saturday.
questions.explanation: We add -s to the verb when the subject is he, she, or it. 'Sister' = she, so we say 'plays'.

questions.question: There ___ many books on the table.
questions.explanation: 'Books' is plural (more than one), so we use 'there are'. 'There is' goes with one thing.

questions.question: I have ___ apple for lunch.
questions.explanation: We use 'an' before words that start with a vowel sound (a, e, i, o, u). 'Apple' starts with 'a'.

questions.question: She is taller ___ her brother.
questions.explanation: We use 'than' to compare two things: taller than, faster than, older than.

questions.question: We don't have ___ milk in the fridge.
questions.explanation: In negative sentences (with 'don't' or 'isn't'), we use 'any' instead of 'some'.

questions.question: If it rains tomorrow, we ___ at home.
questions.explanation: First conditional: If + present simple, will + verb. 'If it rains, we will stay.'

questions.question: He ___ to Spain last summer.
questions.explanation: 'Last summer' is in the past, so we use past simple. 'Go' is irregular: go → went → gone.

questions.question: Look! The cat ___ on the chair.
questions.explanation: 'Look!' tells us it's happening RIGHT NOW. We use present continuous: is/am/are + verb-ing.

questions.question: I ___ never been to London.
questions.explanation: Present perfect = have/has + past participle. With I/you/we/they we use 'have'.

questions.question: Whose pen is this? It's ___.
questions.explanation: After 'It's', we use possessive pronouns: mine, yours, his, hers. 'My' goes BEFORE a noun (my pen).

questions.question: I usually wake ___ at 7 o'clock.
questions.explanation: 'Wake up' is a phrasal verb that means to stop sleeping.

questions.question: She is good ___ playing the piano.
questions.explanation: We say 'good at' something. For example: good at maths, good at football.

questions.question: We are going ___ holiday next week.
questions.explanation: We always say 'on holiday' (British English) or 'on vacation' (American English).

questions.question: My birthday is ___ June.
questions.explanation: We use 'in' for months and years: in June, in 2024. We use 'on' for days and dates.

questions.question: I'm afraid ___ spiders.
questions.explanation: We say 'afraid of' something: afraid of the dark, afraid of dogs.

questions.question: Tom is interested ___ music.
questions.explanation: We say 'interested in' something: interested in sports, interested in art.

questions.question: Please be quiet ___ the library.
questions.explanation: We use 'in' for places with walls: in the library, in the classroom.

questions.question: She arrived ___ school early today.
questions.explanation: We 'arrive at' a place (school, the airport). But we 'arrive in' a city or country.

questions.question: Put the words in the correct order:
questions.explanation: Order: subject (We) + verb (go) + place (to the park) + time (on Sundays).
questions.correctSentence: We go to the park on Sundays

questions.question: Make a yes/no question with these words:
questions.explanation: Yes/No question: Do/Does + subject + verb. 'Do you play...?'
questions.correctSentence: Do you play football at weekends

questions.question: Put the words in the correct order:
questions.explanation: Subject + verb 'be' + age: 'My sister is ten years old.'
questions.correctSentence: My sister is ten years old

questions.question: Put the words in the correct order:
questions.explanation: Adverbs of frequency (always, often, never) usually go BEFORE the main verb: 'I never have'.
questions.correctSentence: I never have cereal for breakfast

questions.question: What does 'huge' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Huge' means very big. Example: 'Elephants are huge animals.'

questions.question: What does 'glad' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Glad' means happy. 'I'm glad to see you!' = 'I'm happy to see you!'

questions.question: What does 'begin' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Begin' and 'start' mean the same thing. The opposite is 'finish' or 'end'.

questions.question: What does 'kind' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Kind' means nice and helpful. A kind person cares about others.

questions.question: Choose the correct form: 'My mum ___ in a hospital.'
questions.explanation: Present simple, 3rd person singular (he/she/it): we add -s. Mum = she, so 'works'.

questions.question: Choose the correct form: 'They ___ in the garden right now.'
questions.explanation: 'Right now' tells us the action is happening now. Present continuous: are + playing.

questions.question: Choose the correct form: 'Yesterday I ___ pizza for dinner.'
questions.explanation: 'Yesterday' = past time. 'Eat' is irregular: eat → ate → eaten. Past simple = ate.

questions.question: Choose the correct form: 'I have ___ my homework.'
questions.explanation: Present perfect: have/has + past participle. The past participle of 'finish' is 'finished'.

questions.question: Look outside! It ___.
questions.explanation: 'Look!' tells us something is happening NOW. We use present continuous: is raining.

questions.question: I always ___ my teeth before bed.
questions.explanation: 'Always' = a habit or routine. We use present simple for habits: I brush, she brushes.

questions.question: When I ___ a baby, I cried a lot.
questions.explanation: With 'I' in past simple of 'be' we use 'was'. (I was, you were, he/she was, we were, they were.)

questions.question: We ___ in this house since 2020.
questions.explanation: 'Since 2020' = from a past time until now. We use present perfect: have lived.

questions.question: Make a question from: 'She likes pizza.'
questions.explanation: For he/she/it questions in present simple: Does + subject + verb (no -s on the main verb).

questions.question: What ___ you doing now?
questions.explanation: 'You doing' is present continuous. We need 'are': 'What are you doing?'

questions.question: ___ you ever been to a zoo?
questions.explanation: 'Ever been' is the present perfect. The question form is 'Have you ever...?'

questions.question: How old is Lily?
questions.explanation: The story says: 'Lily is 11 years old.'
questions.reading: Lily is 11 years old. She lives in a small town with her parents and her dog, Max. Every morning, Lily walks Max in the park before school. Her favourite subject is art because she loves drawing animals. After school, she often visits her grandmother, who lives next door. They bake cookies together every Friday.

questions.question: What is the name of Lily's dog?
questions.explanation: The story tells us: '...her dog, Max.'
questions.reading: Lily is 11 years old. She lives in a small town with her parents and her dog, Max. Every morning, Lily walks Max in the park before school. Her favourite subject is art because she loves drawing animals. After school, she often visits her grandmother, who lives next door. They bake cookies together every Friday.

questions.question: Why does Lily like art?
questions.explanation: The story says: 'Her favourite subject is art because she loves drawing animals.'
questions.reading: Lily is 11 years old. She lives in a small town with her parents and her dog, Max. Every morning, Lily walks Max in the park before school. Her favourite subject is art because she loves drawing animals. After school, she often visits her grandmother, who lives next door. They bake cookies together every Friday.

questions.question: When do Lily and her grandma bake cookies?
questions.explanation: The story tells us: 'They bake cookies together every Friday.'
questions.reading: Lily is 11 years old. She lives in a small town with her parents and her dog, Max. Every morning, Lily walks Max in the park before school. Her favourite subject is art because she loves drawing animals. After school, she often visits her grandmother, who lives next door. They bake cookies together every Friday.

questions.question: I was tired, ___ I went to bed early.
questions.explanation: 'So' shows a result. 'I was tired' is the reason → 'so I went to bed early' is the result.

questions.question: She likes apples ___ she doesn't like oranges.
questions.explanation: 'But' shows a contrast (different ideas). She likes one fruit BUT not the other.

questions.question: We stayed inside ___ it was raining.
questions.explanation: 'Because' gives a reason. Why did we stay inside? Because it was raining.

questions.question: Choose the correct version: 'He ___ like vegetables.'
questions.explanation: With he/she/it we use 'doesn't' (not 'don't'): He doesn't, She doesn't, It doesn't.

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence:
questions.explanation: With he/she/it we say 'has' (not 'have'). I/you/we/they have. He/she/it has.

questions.question: Excuse me, could you ___ me the time, please?
questions.explanation: We 'tell someone the time' (with me/him/her). 'Say' doesn't take a person directly after it.

questions.question: Take an umbrella! It's very ___ today.
questions.explanation: We use umbrellas when it's rainy (when it's raining).

questions.question: I'm hungry. Let's order some ___.
questions.explanation: When we are hungry, we eat food. 'Order food' means ask for food at a restaurant or by phone.

questions.question: On weekends, my hobby is ___ comic books.
questions.explanation: After 'is/was/are' + hobby, we use the -ing form (gerund): My hobby is reading, swimming, dancing.
